steps.content: The equation $21h + 10c = 900$ models total earnings in dollars. Each term is (rate) × (hours), so each coefficient represents a dollar-per-hour rate.
The variable $h$ is defined as hours worked at the regular job. The number 21 is multiplied by $h$, so 21 is the hourly rate (dollars per hour) at the regular job.

Evaluate all choices:
  • A: "The amount, in dollars, the technician earned for each hour she worked at her regular job" — 21 is the coefficient of $h$ (regular job hours), so this is the hourly rate for the regular job. That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
  • The amount, in dollars, the technician earned for each hour she worked at her second job: Assigns 21 to the second job, but 21 multiplies $h$ (regular job), not $c$ (second job). Eliminated.
  • The number of hours the technician worked in one week at her regular job: Interprets 21 as a count of hours, not a rate. Eliminated.
  • The number of hours the technician worked in one week at her second job: Interprets 21 as hours at the second job; 21 doesn't multiply $c$. Eliminated.
